original_abstract: Offloading data traffic from cellular to Wi-Fi serves as a prime example of inter-radio access technology (RAT) coordination. While this strategy clearly mitigates congestion on overloaded cellular networks, the fundamental limits of such offloading and its broader impact on system-wide performance remain poorly understood. To address this gap, we develop a comprehensive and tractable analytical framework comprising $M$ distinct RATs. Each technology features up to $K$ tiers of access points (APs) characterized by unique transmit powers, path loss exponents, deployment densities, and bandwidth allocations. We model each AP class as an independent Poisson point process (PPP) and represent mobile user locations through a separate, independent PPP, with all channels subject to i.i.d. Rayleigh fading. Using this framework, we derive the rate distribution across the entire network under a weighted association strategy, where weights can be adjusted to optimize specific performance metrics. Our analysis reveals that the optimal offloading ratio for maximizing SINR coverage generally differs from the ratio required to maximize rate coverage, defined as the proportion of users achieving a minimum throughput threshold.
humanized_abstract: Offloading data traffic from cellular to Wi-Fi is an example of inter-radio access technology (RAT) coordination. Although it relieves the highly loaded cellular network, the intrinsic bounds of the offloading and the resulting impacts on the overall system performance are not well understood. Motivated by this, we develop a new analytical model that is both practical and accurate for a system that has $M$ number of RATs. Within each RAT, up to $K$ tiers of access points (APs) are characterized by distinct transmit powers, path loss exponents, AP deployment densities and the bandwidth of each tier. Each AP tier is modeled using an independent Poisson point process (PPP) and the locations of mobile users are modeled using an independent PPP. All channels experience independent and identically distributed (i.i.d) Rayleigh fading. Finally, we derive the rate distribution of the overall network while using a weighted association method, where the system weights can be selected based on the desired performance metric. Our results show that the optimal offloading ratios for maximizing the coverage of a target SINR and the coverage of a target rate are different. The former is different from the later by a constant factor that depends on average equals to the square root of the minimum transmit power.
